t: HPPD — antipsychotics usually ineffective; clonazepam helps some patients; long-term monitoring.
(refractory MDD, PTSD) — used under supervision in research protocols; approved protocols in standard clinical practice are still limited (FDA esketamine Spravato is an exception).
NIDA — psychedelic research renaissance; clinical recommendation still pending.
Calm environment, reassurance, benzodiazepine as needed.
Psilocybin + psychotherapy, ketamine + psychotherapy; in clinical trial protocols.
Most users do not develop dependence.
Chronic course in patients with severe psychotic or HPPD complications.
Myth 1: "Hallucinogens are not addictive, they are completely safe"
Physical dependence rare, but psychological dependence and severe psychotic episodes possible; HPPD is a long-term complication.
Myth 2: "Psilocybin therapy is approved for all depression"
still under research phase; not fully approved into standard clinical practice. FDA 'Breakthrough Therapy' status, but broad recommendation premature.
Myth 3: "Ayahuasca is safe for religious ritual"
serotonin syndrome (interaction with SSRI), psychotic decompensation cases have been reported.
Myth 4: "Ketamine is a 'club drug', no medical use"
Ketamine used as anesthetic for decades; intranasal esketamine (Spravato) FDA approved in 2019 for severe refractory depression.
HPPD DSM-5 official diagnosis; some patients have persistent visual disturbances.
WHO. ICD-11. 6C46 Disorders due to use of hallucinogens. 2024.
Halpern J.H., Lerner A.G., Passie T. A review of Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD). Curr Top Behav Neurosci 2018;36:333–360.
Carhart-Harris R.L. et al. Trial of psilocybin versus escitalopram for depression. NEJM 2021;384(15):1402–1411.
